source.title: Caribbean Petroleum Tank Terminal Explosion and Multiple Tank Fires

event.top_event: Tank overfill and vapor cloud explosion
actions_taken: Dock operator halted flow of gasoline to Tank 409
Supervisor notified WWT operator and facility personnel
Security personnel deployed to security gate
Emergency responders from across Puerto Rico and US mainland mobilized
Industrial firefighting company engaged to control fires
Fire suppression operations conducted for 66 hours
event.summary: On October 23, 2009, at approximately 12:23 a.m., a 5-million gallon aboveground storage tank (Tank 409) at the CAPECO facility in Bayamón, Puerto Rico overflowed during offloading of gasoline from the tanker ship Cape Bruny. Approximately 194,000 gallons of gasoline escaped, forming a large vapor cloud that encompassed 107 acres. The vapor cloud migrated through open dike drain valves to low-lying areas and the wastewater treatment area, where it ignited. The resulting explosion registered 2.9 on the Richter scale, damaged approximately 300 homes and businesses up to 1.25 miles away, caused over $5 million in damages to Fort Buchanan military facility, and resulted in fires that burned for nearly 60 hours, destroying or damaging 17 of 48 petroleum storage tanks. Environmental contamination included petroleum products leaking into soil, nearby wetlands, and navigable waterways.
recommendations: EPA should require risk assessments for bulk petroleum storage tank terminals considering proximity to communities and potential for multiple tank releases
OSHA should extend Process Safety Management (PSM) standard to tank terminal facilities
EPA should require high-level alarm systems and automatic overfill prevention systems under SPCC requirements
API should revise ANSI/API 2350 to require automatic overfill prevention systems for all facilities, not just remotely operated ones
NFPA should revise NFPA 30 to require independent or redundant level alarms and automatic overfill prevention systems
ICC should require independent audible or visual alarms to indicate rising liquid levels
Facilities should conduct comprehensive risk assessments considering site complexity, flammable liquid types, and community proximity
Emergency responders should conduct pre-incident planning with facilities for vapor cloud explosions and multiple tank fires
National incident database for tank terminal incidents should be established to assess frequency and develop tailored requirements

hazards.name: Vapor cloud formation from liquid overflow
Ignition source in proximity to vapor cloud

threats.name: Tank overfill due to malfunctioning level gauge
Tank overfill due to increased gasoline flow rate from ship
Tank overfill due to internal floating roof failure
Vapor cloud migration through open dike drain valves
Ignition of vapor cloud in wastewater treatment area

consequences.name: Environmental contamination of soil and waterways
Disruption to air and vehicle transportation

controls.name: Float and tape automatic tank gauge (ATG) system
controls.lod_basis: Primary level measurement system for tank filling operations

supporting_text: On the night of the incident, the transmitter on Tank 409 was not sending level data measurements to the computer.
CAPECO operators often did not rely on the information displayed on the computer because the transmitters were frequently out of service.
Malfunctioning of the tank side gauge or the float and tape apparatus during filling operations led to recording of inaccurate tank levels

controls.name: Manual tank level readings by operators
controls.barrier_role: Verification of tank level during filling
controls.lod_basis: Hourly manual checks of tank level gauge

supporting_text: At the 11 p.m. walk-around, the tank farm operator observed the side gauge on Tank 409 during his hourly check.
The operator called the level into the supervisor who calculated once again that the tank should be full at 1 a.m.; however, between the 11 p.m. and 12 a.m. check, Tank 409 began to overflow.
Insufficient lighting in the tank farm areas hindered operators from observing the overfilling of Tank 409 and the subsequent vapor cloud formation.

controls.name: Manual tank gauging by independent inspector
controls.barrier_role: Verification of tank level before and after filling
controls.lod_basis: Third-party verification of tank levels using gauging tape

supporting_text: Prior to the start and end of filling operations, the independent inspector manually measured the fuel tank levels by lowering a gauging tape into the tank.

The independent inspector and the operator placed car seals on various block valves of receiving tanks to prevent flow into or out of the tank before measuring the level in the tank and recording the readings on a form called a tank gauge ticket.

controls.name: Tank filling procedures and calculations
controls.barrier_role: Determination of tank fill time and termination point
controls.lod_basis: Planning Department calculations based on pipeline capacity and ship discharge volume

supporting_text: Inadequate tank filling procedures.

Normal variations in the gasoline flow rate and pressure from the Cape Bruny without the facility's ability to identify and incorporate the flow rate change in real time into tank fill time calculations may have contributed to the overfill.

CAPECO's normal filling operations required that operators partially open the intake valve to a tank while filling another tank, because the pressure in the pipeline from the dock made manually opening a fully closed valve difficult. This inefficiency increased the potential error in fill time calculations.

controls.name: Secondary containment dike system
controls.lod_basis: Secondary containment to prevent environmental release

supporting_text: The topography of the tank farm allowed the gasoline vapor cloud to migrate through open dike valves to low-lying areas of the tank farm and to the storm water retention pond in the wastewater treatment area, where it ignited.
Fuel gushed from the vents, creating a spray of gasoline that formed a vapor cloud and pooled in the secondary containment dike.

controls.name: Dike drain valve management
controls.lod_basis: Operator inspection and closure of dike valves

supporting_text: The design of the dike valve system made it difficult to distinguish between open and closed valve positions.

Operations staff managed the secondary containment valves that drained storm water through storm water pipes to the storm water retention pond in the WWT plant. The morning operator closed the dike valves after rainstorms, and the evening WWT operator (2 p.m. to 10 p.m.) verified the valves were closed.
Open dike drain valves allowed vapor cloud migration to low-lying areas

controls.name: High-level alarm system
controls.barrier_role: Alert operators to approaching tank maximum level
controls.lod_basis: Independent alarm to warn of overfill condition

supporting_text: Tanks were not equipped with an independent high-level alarm system.
Lack of Additional Safeguards such as High-Level Alarms and an Automatic Overfill Prevention System

A high-level alarm system or high-integrity overfill prevention system are not required by OSHA's Flammable and Combustible Liquids standard, the EPA's Spill Prevention Control and Countermeasure (SPCC) requirements.

controls.name: Automatic Overfill Prevention System (AOPS)
controls.barrier_role: Automatic termination of tank filling at maximum level
controls.lod_basis: Independent system to prevent overfill by stopping flow

supporting_text: Tanks were not equipped with an independent Automatic Overfill Prevention System for terminating transfer operations.
NO REDUNDANT OR INDEPENDENT SAFEGUARDS TO PREVENT OVERFILLING A TANK
ANSI/API 2350 only requires an automatic overfill prevention system for remotely operated facilities and does not offer substantial guidance on conducting a risk assessment

controls.name: Tank farm lighting
controls.barrier_role: Enable operator observation of tank conditions and vapor cloud
controls.lod_basis: Illumination for visual inspection and hazard detection

supporting_text: Insufficient lighting in the tank farm areas hindered operators from observing the overfilling of Tank 409 and the subsequent vapor cloud formation.

Despite the lack of illumination, they observed a white fog approximately three feet above the ground but could not hear or see gasoline overflowing from the vents on Tank 409 due to lack of lighting and the topography of the tank farm.

controls.name: Safety management system and procedures
controls.barrier_role: Establish and enforce safe operating practices
controls.lod_basis: Formal procedures for tank terminal operations

supporting_text: Lack of Formal Procedures for Tank Terminal Operations
Systemic Failure at CAPECO Led to Failure of the Overfill Prevention System
CAPECO History of Poorly Maintaining Terminal Operations
Unreliable Safety Critical Equipment

controls.name: Internal floating roof system
controls.barrier_role: Prevent vapor formation above liquid surface
controls.lod_basis: Floating roof to minimize vapor space

supporting_text: Potential failure of the tank's internal floating roof due to turbulence and other factors may have contributed to the overfill.

controls.name: Risk assessment and hazard analysis
controls.barrier_role: Identify hazards and design appropriate safeguards

supporting_text: NO RISK ASSESSMENT CONSIDERING PROXIMITY TO COMMUNITIES
The CSB finds US regulations fail to consider bulk petroleum storage tank terminals similar to CAPECO as high-hazard facilities.

Due to a lack of regulatory coverage under the Occupational Safety and Health Administration's (OSHA) Process Safety Management (PSM) standard and the Environmental Protection Agency's (EPA) Risk Management Plan (RMP), tank terminal facilities are not required to conduct risk assessments

controls.name: Emergency response planning and training
controls.barrier_role: Respond to and mitigate consequences of vapor cloud explosion

supporting_text: CAPECO and the local fire department lacked sufficient firefighting equipment to effectively fight and control a fire involving multiple tanks because they are not required to conduct a risk analysis where they have to consider and plan for the potential of a vapor cloud explosion involving multiple tanks.
CAPECO did not preplan with local emergency responders or adequately train facility personnel to deal with a fire involving multiple tanks.
Local fire departments lacked sufficient training and resources to respond to industrial fires and explosion.

notes.rules: JSON output only. mentioned fields must be evidence-based. Use null for unknown values.
